title: Westpac brings forward RBA rate cut forecasts, after one more hike
full_text:
That’s all from The Australian’s rolling coverage of business and markets news for Friday, July 10.
Supermarket giant Coles’ talks to buy TPG Capital’s $4bn Petbarn owner Greencross may cost investors, corporate Australia, some say.
Morningstar market strategist Lochlan Halloway’s weekly note on Friday highlighted how listed companies are reaching into private markets too, “buying businesses and carrying them onto the exchange without the ceremony of a float”.
“That doesn’t mean there isn’t a cost to all of this,” he said. The Australian’s Eric Johnston has written about why it may not be such a great move by Coles CEO Leah Weckert.
Coles CEO Leah Weckert. Picture: Martin Keep.
Greencross, the pet-care business that was listed on the ASX until 2019, was first taken private by TPG Capital.
TPG planned to float it again this year for about $4bn, only for Coles to emerge as a potential buyer. Once a standalone business, it could yet return to public markets inside another, much larger listed entity.
Mr Halloway noted that “if Coles absorbs Greencross, an investor who wants pet care could now own it only by buying Coles, and with it groceries and liquor.” “The business survives. What dies is the pure-play, the ability to hold that one exposure on its own.”
He said the deal may not matter so much for an index investor, but may have a “larger implication for corporate Australia”.
“If the route onto the public market increasingly runs through being bought by an incumbent, those companies already big enough to do the buying keep getting bigger, stifling challengers from competing,” he said.
“That might be of some concern in an economy already thick with duopolies and oligopolies, and a matter for the ACCC to watch.”
Coles last at $23.67.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
